question: Explain the primary difference between a stack and a queue data structure, and provide a real-world example for each.
expectedPoints: Key operational principle: Stack is LIFO (Last-In, First-Out); Queue is FIFO (First-In, First-Out).
Real-world stack example: The undo function in an application (last action undone first).
Real-world queue example: A line of customers waiting for service (first customer served first).

question: Compare and contrast the time complexities for insertion, deletion, and access operations in an Array and a Linked List. Justify your reasoning for the key differences.
expectedPoints: Array: O(1) access by index; O(n) worst-case for insertion/deletion at the beginning due to shifting.
Linked List: O(n) access by index (must traverse); O(1) for insertion/deletion at the head (if pointer is maintained).
Justification: The physical memory layout of an array allows instant index calculation, while a linked list's dynamic pointers allow efficient restructuring without shifting elements.

question: Describe the process of inserting a new node into a Binary Search Tree (BST). Then, analyze the best-case and worst-case time complexity for this operation, explaining what tree structure leads to each scenario.
expectedPoints: Process: Start at root; compare new node's key with current node; go left if smaller, right if larger; repeat until a null child pointer is found, then insert.
Best-case time complexity (O(log n)): Occurs in a balanced tree (height is log2(n)), halving the search space each step.
Worst-case time complexity (O(n)): Occurs in a degenerate/unbalanced tree (essentially a linked list), requiring a traversal of all n nodes.
